prompt.text: Centrosome movement, spindle formation, and nuclear envelope breakdown.
Chromosomes condense progressively.
Homologous chromosomes pairs are aligned and chromosomal cross over occurs.
Chiasmatas first appear.
Microtubules from opposite poles attach to the kinetochores, one at the center of each homolog.
Pairs of homologous chromosomes are aligned at the metaphase plate.
Each homologous pair is aligned at the metaphase plate independent of the other pairs.
Both chromatids of one homolog are attached to kinetochore of their nearest centrosomal pole.
Breakdown of proteins along chromatid arms allows homologs to separate.
Homologs move to opposite poles, guided by spindle apparatus.
Sister chromatids are still joined at the centromere, causing them to move as a unit.
At the start, each pole has a complete haploid set of duplicated chromosomes.
Cytokinesis forms two haploid daughter cells.
Two haploid daughter cells have formed, but no chromosomal duplication occurs.
Spindle apparatus forms in a haploid cell.
In the late stage, sister chromatids are moved via microtubules to the metaphase plate.
Sister chromatids are positioned at the metaphase plate.
Breakdown of proteins holding sister chromatids together, allowing them to separate and move to opposite poles.
Each chromatid is now an independent chromosome in a haploid cell.
Nuclei form and chromosomes begin condensing.
Following cytokinesis, 4 genetically distinct haploid cells have formed.
